coverage.detail: The product should answer only through bounded repository capabilities within explicit allowlisted scope. It should return summaries or drafts rather than raw repository export, ask for clarification when repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, stop before executing issue creation, PR comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without approval, and deny unsafe requests such as secret access, unbounded export, raw file exfiltration, or workflow bypass. Governed actions should remain auditable.
coverage.label: Governed Behavior Summary
coverage.mapping_note: This PM governed behavior summary is formalized explicitly in Product Intent Formalization on the Service Formalization page.
coverage.mapping_target_label: Developer Design > Service Formalization > Product Intent Formalization > Governed Behavior Summary

coverage.detail: Read-oriented repository search and inspection can proceed within bounded scope, while issue creation, pull request comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication must stop at explicit approval boundaries. Drafting and preview are allowed where the brief marks them as preview-only or draft-only, but execution is not automatic.
coverage.label: Approval Posture Summary
coverage.mapping_note: This PM approval posture is formalized explicitly in Product Intent Formalization on the Service Formalization page.
coverage.mapping_target_label: Developer Design > Service Formalization > Product Intent Formalization > Approval Posture Summary

patches.rationale: Directly supported by the stated purpose and fronting intent: governed repository capabilities instead of raw GitHub operations, including inspection, search, issue preview, approved issue creation intent, governed mutation boundaries, workflow requests, and release-note drafting.
patches.value: Provide business-safe, governed access to GitHub repository and delivery operations through bounded capabilities that let users inspect repository context, search issues and pull requests, prepare changes for review, request approved actions, and draft release notes without exposing raw code-hosting tools directly to agents.

patches.rationale: The source repeatedly frames the problem as avoiding broad tool exposure and replacing it with reviewed capabilities, explicit approvals, denial rules, repository allowlists, and audit.
patches.value: Raw GitHub tools are powerful but too broad for safe agent use. Teams need a governed fronting layer that limits repository scope, makes approval requirements explicit, blocks unsafe operations, and preserves auditability while still enabling common repository and delivery tasks.

patches.rationale: These goals are explicitly stated or strongly implied across the purpose, service boundary, governed capability surface, policy semantics, and fronting intent.
value: Expose business-safe repository and issue capabilities instead of raw GitHub API operations.
Make allowed organization and repository scope explicit and enforceable.

Support repository inspection, issue and pull request context search, issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, workflow dispatch requests, and release-note drafting within bounded controls.
Require explicit approval before higher-impact mutations or execution steps.
Deny unsafe behaviors such as secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and workflow bypass.
Preserve approval records and audit metadata for governed actions.

patches.rationale: These are stable user task families directly reflected in the governed capability surface and backend evidence.
value: Issue and pull request search summary

patches.rationale: This consolidates the policy semantics, outcome posture column, and service boundary controls into a PM-readable behavior summary.
patches.value: The product should answer only through bounded repository capabilities within explicit allowlisted scope. It should return summaries or drafts rather than raw repository export, ask for clarification when repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, stop before executing issue creation, PR comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without approval, and deny unsafe requests such as secret access, unbounded export, raw file exfiltration, or workflow bypass. Governed actions should remain auditable.

patches.rationale: The source clearly distinguishes read-only behavior from preview-only, draft-only, and approval-gated actions.
patches.value: Read-oriented repository search and inspection can proceed within bounded scope, while issue creation, pull request comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication must stop at explicit approval boundaries. Drafting and preview are allowed where the brief marks them as preview-only or draft-only, but execution is not automatic.

patches.rationale: The user requested lockable output when supported. The source is specific enough on purpose, scope, approvals, denials, clarification behavior, and audit expectations to support a lockable PM summary.

patches.value: GitHub access should be governed through bounded ANIP capabilities rather than raw GitHub APIs. Repository scope must be explicit and allowlisted. Repository context search and release-note drafting return bounded results only. Issue preparation and pull-request comment preparation are preview-only and require approval before any downstream mutation. Workflow dispatch requests are approval-gated, and restricted workflows require explicit grant. Missing repository, pull request, workflow, or range context returns clarification_required. Secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and direct workflow bypass are denied. Audit records and approval records must be explicit.

items.body: The product shall provide an ANIP fronting service for GitHub that exposes business-safe repository capabilities rather than raw GitHub REST, GraphQL, or tool-level operations. The contract shall own the capability semantics presented to agents and callers, while native GitHub APIs remain the backend integration surface.
items.rationale: Both sources explicitly position the product as a governed fronting layer that hides broad code-hosting tools behind bounded capabilities.
items.title: Expose a governed GitHub capability surface instead of raw GitHub operations

items.body: The product shall support read-only repository context access for allowed repositories, including bounded issue and pull request search using explicit repository inputs such as owner, repo, and query. Results shall return bounded summaries rather than raw repository export or unbounded codebase data.
items.rationale: The source defines repository search context as a core governed capability and states that repository scope must be explicit and allowlisted.
items.title: Support bounded repository context search and inspection within explicit repository scope

items.body: The product shall allow a caller to prepare a GitHub issue using explicit repository scope and issue fields, including title and body, with governed optional fields such as labels, assignees, and milestone. The preparation step shall return a preview suitable for review and approval rather than directly creating the issue.
items.rationale: The capability table explicitly defines issue preparation as preview-only and maps it to issue creation fields.
items.title: Provide issue preparation as a preview-first flow with governed inputs

items.body: The product shall not execute issue creation, pull request comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without explicit approval. For mutation requests, the system shall stop at a reviewable preview or request state until approval is granted, and shall preserve the approval boundary in the contract behavior.
items.rationale: Approval gating is a central policy semantic in the source and is repeated across issue, PR comment, workflow, and release behaviors.
items.title: Require explicit approval before any governed GitHub mutation is executed

items.body: The product shall support preparation of pull request comments from review or release context using explicit repository and pull request identifiers. The output shall be a preview-only comment draft, with posting blocked until approval is granted.
items.rationale: The source defines a dedicated PR comment preparation capability with preview-only posture and approval required before posting.
items.title: Support governed pull request comment preparation without direct posting

items.body: The product shall support requesting a GitHub Actions workflow run using explicit repository, workflow, and ref inputs, but shall treat dispatch as approval-gated. Workflows designated as restricted shall require explicit grant handling beyond the base request flow, and the system shall not allow workflow bypass through backend options or direct backend access.
items.rationale: The source explicitly defines workflow dispatch as approval-gated and notes that restricted workflows require explicit grant.
items.title: Gate workflow dispatch requests and restrict sensitive workflows

items.body: The product shall support drafting release notes from bounded repository context such as issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data using an explicit release range. The capability shall produce draft output only and shall not create or publish a GitHub release.
items.rationale: The source names release-note draft generation as a backend-supported use case and explicitly states that the governed capability is draft-only.
items.title: Generate release notes as bounded drafts without publishing releases

items.body: The product shall return clarification_required when a request lacks the repository, pull request, workflow, or release range context needed to safely execute the governed capability. The system shall not infer missing scope or proceed with partial context for governed operations.
items.rationale: The policy semantics section explicitly requires clarification_required for missing repo, pull request, workflow, or range context.
items.title: Return clarification_required when required repository or operation context is missing

items.body: The product shall explicitly deny secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumps, arbitrary mutation payload passthrough, and direct workflow bypass. These denials shall be part of the governed contract rather than left to backend behavior alone.
items.rationale: The source lists denied behaviors and frames ANIP as the layer that turns broad GitHub access into reviewed, bounded capabilities.
items.title: Deny unsafe or out-of-bounds GitHub operations at the contract level

items.body: If backend_options are exposed, they shall be limited to bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, or expand-like metadata. backend_options shall not permit arbitrary mutation payloads, secret access, workflow bypass, or unbounded data extraction.
items.rationale: The source gives a precise boundary for backend_options and explicitly forbids using them as a bypass channel.
items.title: Constrain backend options to bounded provider features only

items.body: The product shall make allowed organization and repository scope explicit and enforce it consistently across all governed capabilities. The service design shall reflect centralized enterprise identity, repository allowlists, approval records, and audit expectations as first-class control mechanisms.
items.rationale: The service boundary section and fronting intent both call out enterprise identity, repo allowlists, approval records, and audit.
items.title: Make repository allowlists and enterprise control posture explicit

items.body: The product shall preserve audit metadata that allows reviewers to understand what capability was requested, what repository scope applied, whether the request was previewed, approved, denied, clarified, or executed, and which backend GitHub operation was used. Audit behavior shall be explicit in the contract and service expectations.
items.rationale: Audit is repeatedly named in the source, but the exact metadata schema is not specified, so the requirement is strong while details remain open.
items.title: Record audit metadata for previews, approvals, denials, and backend execution

items.body: The product shall use native GitHub REST and GraphQL APIs as the execution binding for this package. MCP may be referenced only as a comparison point for raw tool exposure and shall not be treated as the required ANIP execution interface.
items.rationale: The source is explicit that native REST/GraphQL is the execution binding and that MCP is comparison-only.
items.title: Bind execution to native GitHub REST and GraphQL integrations, not MCP

questions_for_user: Which organizations and repositories must be allowlisted at launch, and who owns that allowlist?
What approval workflow is required for issue creation, PR comment posting, and workflow dispatch requests?
Should approved issue creation be included in the first release, or should the first release stop at preview-only preparation?
What audit metadata must be retained for previews, approvals, denials, and executed GitHub actions?
Are release publication and other write operations intentionally out of scope for this showcase, or do you expect later expansion?

MCP is explicitly comparison-only in the source and should not be treated as the execution interface for this package.
Missing repository, pull request, workflow, or release range context should produce clarification_required rather than inferred execution.

# Source 1: GitHub governed fronting source specification
Kind: API docs
Filename: source-spec.md

# GitHub Governed Fronting Showcase Source Specification

This source document models a realistic fronting use case: GitHub REST/GraphQL APIs exist downstream, but agents should only see governed repository capabilities.

## Purpose

Demonstrate that ANIP can govern repository and delivery operations without exposing broad code-hosting tools directly to agents.

The ANIP contract owns the capability semantics. Native GitHub APIs are the backend because they are durable service integration surfaces. GitHub MCP may be useful as a comparison point for raw tool exposure, but this package does not use MCP as the ANIP execution interface.

## Service Boundary

- Service ID: `github-governance-service`
- Service name: GitHub Governance Service
- Primary backend: native GitHub REST/GraphQL API adapter
- Deployment posture: centralized ANIP fronting service with enterprise identity, repo allowlists, approval records, and audit.

## Backend Evidence

Native GitHub API supply:

- Repository issue and pull request search through REST or GraphQL.
- Issue preview mapped to issue creation fields.
- Pull request comment preview mapped to review/comment APIs.
- Workflow dispatch request mapped to GitHub Actions workflow dispatch.
- Release-note draft generation from issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data.

MCP comparison:

- GitHub MCP may expose repository search, issue, pull request, workflow, and release operations.
- This showcase intentionally does not bind ANIP capabilities to GitHub MCP tools. The agent-facing surface remains ANIP capabilities backed by bounded GitHub API operations.

## Governed Capability Surface

| Capability | User intent | Required inputs | Optional governed inputs | Outcome posture |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `github.repo.search_context` | Search bounded issue and pull request context in an allowed repository. | `owner`, `repo`, `query` | `state`, `labels`, `limit`, `backend_options` | Read-only. Returns bounded summaries, not raw repository export. |
| `github.issue.prepare` | Prepare an issue with labels and assignees. | `owner`, `repo`, `title`, `body` | `labels`, `assignees`, `milestone`, `backend_options` | Preview-only. Requires approval before creation. |
| `github.pr.comment.prepare` | Prepare a pull request comment from review or release context. | `owner`, `repo`, `pull_number`, `comment_purpose`, `context` | `visibility`, `backend_options` | Preview-only. Requires approval before posting. |
| `github.workflow.dispatch.request` | Request a GitHub Actions workflow run. | `owner`, `repo`, `workflow_id`, `ref` | `inputs`, `backend_options` | Approval-gated. Restricted workflows require explicit grant. |
| `github.release_notes.prepare` | Draft release notes from bounded repository context. | `owner`, `repo`, `range` | `audience`, `include_prs`, `backend_options` | Draft-only. Does not create or publish a release. |

`backend_options` is allowed only for bounded provider features such as selected fields, GraphQL pagination, or expand-like metadata. It must not allow arbitrary mutation payloads, secret access, workflow bypass, or unbounded repository export.

## Policy Semantics

- Repository scope is explicit and allowlisted.
- Missing repo, pull request, workflow, or range context returns `clarification_required`.
- Issue creation, PR comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication are not executed without approval.
- Secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and direct workflow bypass are denied.
- Native REST/GraphQL is the execution binding for this package. MCP can be used as a comparison surface, but is not required for ANIP.

## Why ANIP Helps

GitHub MCP gives agents access to powerful tools. ANIP turns that access into reviewed capabilities with explicit approvals, denial rules, clarification behavior, and audit records.

---

# Source 2: GitHub Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 fronting intent
Kind: Fronting intent
Filename: developer-tools-fronting-intent.md

# GitHub Fronting Showcase 0.2.0 Fronting Intent

Create an ANIP governed fronting service for GitHub. The service should expose business-safe repository and issue capabilities instead of raw GitHub API operations. It must support repository inspection, issue search, issue preview, approved issue creation, and governed mutation boundaries. The contract must make allowed organization/repository scope, approval requirements, denied operations, audit metadata, backend GitHub operations, and live adapter expectations explicit.

## Next review step
Add integration evidence from a real source before contract generation. Use uploaded OpenAPI, GraphQL, MCP schema, API docs, permission matrices, live discovery, or manual operation entry. Studio must not invent backend operations from the project name or domain.

data.policy_summary: GitHub access should be governed through bounded ANIP capabilities rather than raw GitHub APIs. Repository scope must be explicit and allowlisted. Repository context search and release-note drafting return bounded results only. Issue preparation and pull-request comment preparation are preview-only and require approval before any downstream mutation. Workflow dispatch requests are approval-gated, and restricted workflows require explicit grant. Missing repository, pull request, workflow, or range context returns clarification_required. Secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and direct workflow bypass are denied. Audit records and approval records must be explicit.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded issue and pull-request context search within an explicit allowlisted repository. Return summaries only, not raw repository export.
rules.notes: Supported by github.repo.search_context and the read-only bounded search posture.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow issue preparation preview with labels and assignees, but stop for approval before any issue is created downstream.
rules.notes: Preview-only posture from github.issue.prepare; creation is not executed without approval.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow pull-request comment preparation preview from review or release context, but stop for approval before posting any comment.
rules.notes: Preview-only posture from github.pr.comment.prepare; posting is not executed without approval.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow a workflow dispatch request to be prepared for an allowlisted repository and ref, but require approval before dispatch. Restricted workflows require explicit grant.
rules.notes: Supported by github.workflow.dispatch.request and policy semantics for restricted workflows.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow draft release notes from bounded repository context only. Do not create or publish a release.
rules.notes: Supported by github.release_notes.prepare and the draft-only posture.

rules.governed_outcome: Deny requests for secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, or direct workflow bypass.
rules.notes: Cross-cutting denial posture stated in policy semantics.

rules.governed_outcome: Stop and request clarification when repository context is missing or repository scope is not explicit.
rules.notes: The source requires explicit repository scope and clarification on missing context.

data.approval_posture_summary: Read-oriented repository search and inspection can proceed within bounded scope, while issue creation, pull request comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication must stop at explicit approval boundaries. Drafting and preview are allowed where the brief marks them as preview-only or draft-only, but execution is not automatic.

business_goals: Expose business-safe repository and issue capabilities instead of raw GitHub API operations.
Make allowed organization and repository scope explicit and enforceable.

Support repository inspection, issue and pull request context search, issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, workflow dispatch requests, and release-note drafting within bounded controls.
Require explicit approval before higher-impact mutations or execution steps.
Deny unsafe behaviors such as secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and workflow bypass.
Preserve approval records and audit metadata for governed actions.
data.business_problem: Raw GitHub tools are powerful but too broad for safe agent use. Teams need a governed fronting layer that limits repository scope, makes approval requirements explicit, blocks unsafe operations, and preserves auditability while still enabling common repository and delivery tasks.
data.governed_behavior_summary: The product should answer only through bounded repository capabilities within explicit allowlisted scope. It should return summaries or drafts rather than raw repository export, ask for clarification when repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, stop before executing issue creation, PR comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without approval, and deny unsafe requests such as secret access, unbounded export, raw file exfiltration, or workflow bypass. Governed actions should remain auditable.
data.product_purpose: Provide business-safe, governed access to GitHub repository and delivery operations through bounded capabilities that let users inspect repository context, search issues and pull requests, prepare changes for review, request approved actions, and draft release notes without exposing raw code-hosting tools directly to agents.
supported_question_families: Issue and pull request search summary

entries.evidence: Representative flow review shows issue and comment actions produce previews first, workflow dispatches require approval, and no governed mutation executes without an approval step.

entries.statement: Reviewers can inspect approval records and audit metadata for governed repository operations so they can understand what was requested, what was approved or denied, and within which repository scope.

items.body: Require explicit repository-specific inputs before execution or preview generation. For repository search require owner, repo, and query. For issue preparation require owner, repo, title, and body. For pull request comment preparation require owner, repo, pull_number, comment_purpose, and context. For workflow dispatch requests require owner, repo, workflow_id, and ref. For release-note drafting require owner, repo, and range. If required repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, ambiguous, or unresolved, return clarification_required and do not execute backend operations. Do not infer missing scope or widen repository context implicitly. Enforce repository allowlists before any backend call or preview generation.
items.rationale: The baseline repeatedly requires explicit repository scope, consistent clarification_required handling, and allowlist enforcement across the governed GitHub surface.
items.title: Bind explicit repository scope and clarification_required behavior across all capabilities

items.body: Treat issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, and workflow dispatch request as governed prepare-only flows. Runtime policy should allow generation of a reviewable preview or request artifact, produce approval.request and preview-oriented outputs, and explicitly block approval.execute and downstream mutation until approval is granted. Issue preparation must stop before issue creation. Pull request comment preparation must stop before posting. Workflow dispatch requests must stop before dispatch. Approval state and repository scope must remain attached to the runtime outcome for later execution and audit.
items.rationale: The runtime governance evidence and source specification define these capabilities as preview-only or approval-gated and explicitly prohibit direct mutation without approval.
items.title: Preserve preview-first and approval-gated mutation boundaries

items.body: For workflow dispatch requests, apply a second policy check after repository allowlist validation to determine whether the requested workflow is restricted. Restricted workflows must require explicit grant handling beyond the base approval flow. If the request attempts to bypass workflow restrictions through backend_options, alternate workflow identifiers, or direct backend semantics, deny the request at the capability layer. Runtime outcomes should distinguish normal approval-gated workflow requests from restricted-workflow requests that need additional grant validation.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly calls out restricted workflows requiring explicit grant handling and denies direct workflow bypass, but does not fully specify the grant authority.
items.title: Add stricter runtime handling for restricted workflows and workflow bypass attempts

items.body: Define explicit deny outcomes for secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumps, arbitrary mutation payload passthrough, and direct workflow bypass. Denied requests must not be translated into raw GitHub REST or GraphQL calls. The runtime policy should preserve the bounded capability surface even when downstream GitHub APIs could technically perform broader actions. Return a governed stop with denial metadata rather than partial unsafe output.
items.rationale: Unsafe and out-of-bounds operations are explicitly denied in the locked baseline and denial is required to happen at the ANIP contract layer, not only in backend behavior.
items.title: Deny unsafe GitHub operations at the contract layer

items.body: Allow backend_options only for bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, GraphQL pagination, or expand-like metadata. Runtime policy must reject backend_options that attempt arbitrary mutation payloads, secret access, workflow bypass, unbounded extraction, or other raw backend control. Apply the same guardrail consistently across repository search, issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, workflow dispatch requests, and release-note drafting.
items.rationale: The source specification and requirements explicitly allow backend_options only as bounded provider controls and identify them as a potential escape hatch that must be closed.
items.title: Constrain backend_options to bounded provider features only

items.body: For repository search, runtime policy should permit bounded issue and pull request summaries only and prohibit raw repository export or unbounded data return. For release-note drafting, runtime policy should permit bounded aggregation from issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data and return a draft only. Release-note preparation must not create or publish a release. Both capabilities should remain read-oriented even when backed by multiple GitHub data sources.
items.rationale: The baseline defines repository search as bounded summary access and release-note generation as draft-only without release publication.
items.title: Bind read-only and draft-only output posture for search and release-note capabilities

items.body: Attach durable audit metadata to every governed runtime outcome, including requested capability, repository scope, whether the request was previewed, clarified, denied, approved, or executed, and which backend GitHub operation was used. Preserve lineage fields including task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id. Audit records should make approval boundaries and denied or clarified stops reviewable after the fact.
items.rationale: The locked baseline requires durable searchable audit, explicit outcome recording, backend operation traceability, and lineage preservation.
items.title: Record audit and lineage metadata for previews, approvals, denials, clarifications, and execution

items.body: Runtime policy should declare native GitHub REST and GraphQL operations as the only execution binding for this package. MCP may be referenced for comparison but must not be treated as the runtime execution interface or as an alternate path around governed capability semantics. All governed outcomes should resolve through the centralized GitHub fronting service and its native GitHub adapter path.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly states that native GitHub REST/GraphQL is the execution binding and that MCP is not the required ANIP interface.
items.title: Bind runtime execution to native GitHub REST and GraphQL integrations only

questions_for_user: Should approval be modeled as a generic one-time approval only, or do you want named approver classes for issue creation, PR comment posting, and workflow dispatch?
Do you want restricted workflow handling captured as a separate runtime policy block from normal workflow dispatch approval?
Should repository allowlists be represented only as required scope checks, or also as explicit deny outcomes when owner/repo is outside allowed scope?
Do you want backend_options guardrails expressed as one shared cross-capability policy or repeated per capability for stricter runtime generation?

items.body: Define the GitHub Governance Service backend binding target as a native GitHub REST/GraphQL API adapter. The binding should explicitly exclude GitHub MCP as an execution interface. The adapter is the only downstream integration surface for repository context search, issue preview preparation, pull request comment preview preparation, workflow dispatch request preparation, and release-note draft preparation.
items.rationale: The locked baseline and source specification explicitly require native GitHub REST/GraphQL bindings and explicitly position MCP only as a comparison surface.
items.title: Bind governed capabilities to native GitHub REST/GraphQL adapter only

items.body: Bind github.repo.search_context to read-only GitHub repository issue and pull request search operations through REST or GraphQL. The binding should require owner, repo, and query before execution; apply optional state, labels, limit, and bounded backend_options; and return bounded summaries only. The binding must not support raw file export, unbounded repository export, secret access, or codebase dump behavior.
items.rationale: The source defines this capability as atomic, read-only, bounded, and explicitly limited to issue and pull request context summaries within repository scope.
items.title: Map repository context search to bounded read-only GitHub operations

items.body: Bind github.issue.prepare to an adapter operation that prepares issue creation fields and returns a governed preview. The binding should accept owner, repo, title, body, and optional labels, assignees, milestone, and bounded backend_options. It should produce preview content and approval request metadata only, with no direct issue creation call on this path.
items.rationale: Runtime governance marks this capability as prepare_only and preview-producing, and the baseline requires explicit approval before any issue mutation executes.
items.title: Bind issue preparation to preview generation, not issue creation

items.body: Bind github.pr.comment.prepare to an adapter operation that gathers bounded pull request context and prepares a comment preview. The binding should require owner, repo, pull_number, comment_purpose, and context, with optional visibility and bounded backend_options. It must stop at preview and approval request output and must not post a comment on the preparation path.
items.rationale: The source and runtime governance both define this capability as preview-only, approval-gated, and not a direct posting operation.
items.title: Bind pull request comment preparation to preview-only comment drafting

items.body: Bind github.workflow.dispatch.request to an adapter operation that validates repository, workflow_id, ref, and optional inputs, then produces a workflow dispatch preview or request record for approval. The binding should include restricted-workflow evaluation before any downstream dispatch path is eligible. It must deny workflow bypass attempts and must not dispatch on the request-stage binding itself.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly requires approval gating, explicit grant handling for restricted workflows, and denial of workflow bypass through backend options or direct backend access.
items.title: Bind workflow dispatch to request-stage preparation with restricted-workflow checks

items.body: Bind github.release_notes.prepare to adapter operations that gather bounded issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data for an explicit repository and range. The binding should support optional audience, include_prs, and bounded backend_options, and return a draft only. It must not create or publish a GitHub release.
items.rationale: The source explicitly defines release-note generation as draft-only and backed by bounded repository context rather than release publication.
items.title: Bind release-note preparation to bounded repository context aggregation

items.body: Add a shared backend binding rule that required context validation happens in the GitHub Governance Service before adapter invocation. Missing owner, repo, query, title, body, pull_number, workflow_id, ref, or range should return clarification_required with the minimum missing fields, and no downstream GitHub call should occur until the request is complete.
items.rationale: The baseline and scenarios consistently require clarification_required behavior across capabilities when repository-specific context is incomplete.
items.title: Enforce pre-execution clarification gates in the service binding layer

items.body: Define a shared binding guardrail block that rejects secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumps, arbitrary mutation payload passthrough, and direct workflow bypass before any GitHub adapter call is made. backend_options should be constrained to bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, or expand-like metadata only.
items.rationale: The locked requirements make these denials and backend_options constraints part of the governed contract, not optional adapter behavior.
items.title: Apply contract-level denial and backend_options guardrails before adapter calls

items.body: Require every governed backend interaction and governed stop condition to emit durable, searchable audit metadata. The binding should capture capability requested, repository scope, preview versus denial versus clarification versus approval versus execution outcome, backend operation used, and lineage fields including task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id.
items.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage fields are explicit locked requirements, and the baseline requires audit evidence for previews, approvals, denials, and backend execution.
items.title: Bind all GitHub adapter calls to durable audit and lineage capture

questions_for_user: Should backend bindings name concrete GitHub REST endpoints and GraphQL queries now, or stay at adapter-operation level for this review pass?
Do you want a separate binding block for approval execution after preview, or should this proposal stay limited to preview/request-stage bindings only?
Should restricted workflow grant evaluation be modeled as part of the same service binding block or as a distinct policy dependency block?
Do you want repository allowlist and catalog resolvers treated as backend dependencies in this artifact, or left to a separate developer design section?
envelope.summary: Candidate backend binding blocks for the single public GitHub governance service. The source is explicit that ANIP capabilities bind to native GitHub REST/GraphQL APIs, preserve approval and denial boundaries, and keep repository scope, audit, and clarification behavior in the fronting layer.
envelope.title: Backend Binding Proposal: GitHub Governed Fronting Service
watchouts: The locked baseline says native GitHub REST/GraphQL is the execution binding; do not introduce MCP as a runtime binding.
Preview-only capabilities must not be bound directly to mutation execution paths in a way that bypasses approval.
backend_options must remain bounded to provider-safe features such as field selection or pagination and must not become a passthrough for arbitrary payloads.
Clarification-required behavior must happen before backend execution when required repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing.
Repository allowlist enforcement and denial rules belong in the governed fronting layer, not only in downstream adapter behavior.
Audit lineage should preserve task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id across preview, denial, clarification, approval, and execution stages.

Evidence should show that secret access, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumping, arbitrary mutation passthrough, and workflow bypass are explicitly denied at the contract layer rather than relying on backend failure.
items.rationale: The baseline and denial scenario make contract-level denial and bounded capability surface core product intent.
items.title: Verify the governed capability boundary stays narrower than native GitHub power

items.body: Verification should confirm that requests missing required repository, pull request, workflow, or release range context stop before backend execution and return clarification_required with only the minimum missing inputs requested. Coverage should include repository search, issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, workflow dispatch request, and release-note drafting, with no silent defaults that widen scope or guess identifiers.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly standardizes clarification behavior across capabilities and forbids inferred scope.
items.title: Verify missing required context consistently returns clarification_required

items.body: Verification should prove that owner and repo inputs are required where specified, resolved against the governed repository scope, and rejected or stopped when outside the allowed repository posture. Evidence should show that read and preview flows remain bounded to the requested allowlisted repository and do not leak cross-repository context.
items.rationale: Repository allowlists and explicit repository scope are first-class controls in the locked baseline and source specification.
items.title: Verify explicit repository scope and allowlist enforcement before execution

items.body: Verification should confirm that issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, and workflow dispatch request produce preview or request artifacts suitable for review and do not perform downstream mutation during the initial capability call. Expected evidence includes preview-shaped outputs, explicit approval-needed posture, and absence of issue creation, comment posting, or workflow dispatch side effects before approval.
items.rationale: The runtime governance worksheet marks these capabilities as prepare_only with approval-request output and no execution at initial call time.
items.title: Verify mutation-oriented capabilities stop at preview or request state before execution

items.body: Verification should prove that workflow dispatch requests require explicit repository, workflow, and ref inputs, stop for approval before dispatch, and apply stricter handling when the targeted workflow is restricted. Coverage should include denial of workflow bypass attempts and rejection of backend option usage that could circumvent workflow policy.
items.rationale: Workflow dispatch is the only capability with an explicit restricted-workflow grant rule beyond standard approval gating.
items.title: Verify workflow dispatch requests enforce restricted-workflow controls

items.body: Verification should confirm that repository search returns bounded issue and pull request summaries only, and that release-note generation returns a bounded draft only. Evidence should show that neither capability exposes raw repository export, unbounded codebase data, secret material, or release publication behavior.
items.rationale: The baseline distinguishes bounded summaries and drafts from raw export and direct publication.
items.title: Verify read and draft capabilities return bounded outputs rather than raw exports

items.body: Verification should check that required inputs are enforced, optional defaults are applied only where specified, and closed-value fields reject or clarify unsupported values. Coverage should include search state defaulting to open, pull request comment visibility defaulting to internal, release note audience defaulting to engineering, include_prs defaulting to true, and clarification behavior for unresolved entity references such as repositories, labels, assignees, milestones, pull requests, and workflows.
items.rationale: The runtime input worksheets provide concrete developer-facing contract evidence that should be reflected in verification.
items.title: Verify input handling matches the reviewed capability input contracts

items.body: Verification should include negative tests showing that backend_options can support bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, or expand-like metadata, but cannot introduce arbitrary mutation payloads, secret access, workflow bypass, or unbounded extraction. Evidence should show that backend_options never become an escape hatch around the governed contract.
items.rationale: backend_options guardrails are repeated across the baseline, source specification, and capability contracts.
items.title: Verify backend_options remain bounded provider features only

items.body: Verification should prove that previews, clarifications, denials, approvals, and backend execution events record durable, searchable audit metadata with enough detail to reconstruct requested capability, repository scope, outcome posture, and backend operation used. Where available, evidence should also preserve task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id lineage fields.
items.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage fields are explicit locked requirements and central to governance credibility.
items.title: Verify audit and lineage evidence is recorded for governed outcomes

items.body: Verification should confirm that the package binds governed capabilities to native GitHub REST or GraphQL backend operations and does not depend on MCP as the execution interface. Evidence can reference backend operation mappings for repository context search, issue preview preparation, pull request comment preview preparation, workflow dispatch request preparation, and release-note drafting.
items.rationale: The source specification explicitly treats native GitHub APIs as the execution binding and MCP only as comparison context.
items.title: Verify execution binding stays on native GitHub REST or GraphQL integrations

questions_for_user: Do you want verification expectations split by capability, or kept as cross-cutting blocks for the whole GitHub Governance Service?
Should approval execution after preview be covered in this pass, or only preview/request-side behavior that is explicit in the current baseline?
Do you want audit verification to require exact recorded fields, or only presence of request scope, outcome, and backend operation evidence?
envelope.summary: Candidate developer-facing verification expectation blocks derived from the locked baseline, source specification, and runtime evidence. Focus is on proving governed GitHub behavior, approval boundaries, clarification stops, deny rules, bounded outputs, and audit coverage.
envelope.title: Verification Expectations Proposal: GitHub Fronting Showcase 0.2.0
watchouts: The baseline is explicit about preview, approval, denial, clarification, and bounded output posture; verification should not expand the product into raw GitHub tool coverage.
Workflow dispatch has an extra restricted-workflow grant rule; do not treat it as equivalent to ordinary approval-gated mutations.
Release notes are draft-only and read-oriented; verification should not imply release creation or publication paths.
backend_options are allowed only as bounded provider features; verification should include escape-hatch checks, not just happy-path input acceptance.
The locked capability inventory must remain exactly the five governed capabilities in source evidence.

accepted_payload.body: Define the GitHub Governance Service backend binding target as a native GitHub REST/GraphQL API adapter. The binding should explicitly exclude GitHub MCP as an execution interface. The adapter is the only downstream integration surface for repository context search, issue preview preparation, pull request comment preview preparation, workflow dispatch request preparation, and release-note draft preparation.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline and source specification explicitly require native GitHub REST/GraphQL bindings and explicitly position MCP only as a comparison surface.
accepted_payload.title: Bind governed capabilities to native GitHub REST/GraphQL adapter only

accepted_payload.body: Bind github.repo.search_context to read-only GitHub repository issue and pull request search operations through REST or GraphQL. The binding should require owner, repo, and query before execution; apply optional state, labels, limit, and bounded backend_options; and return bounded summaries only. The binding must not support raw file export, unbounded repository export, secret access, or codebase dump behavior.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source defines this capability as atomic, read-only, bounded, and explicitly limited to issue and pull request context summaries within repository scope.
accepted_payload.title: Map repository context search to bounded read-only GitHub operations

accepted_payload.body: Bind github.issue.prepare to an adapter operation that prepares issue creation fields and returns a governed preview. The binding should accept owner, repo, title, body, and optional labels, assignees, milestone, and bounded backend_options. It should produce preview content and approval request metadata only, with no direct issue creation call on this path.
accepted_payload.rationale: Runtime governance marks this capability as prepare_only and preview-producing, and the baseline requires explicit approval before any issue mutation executes.
accepted_payload.title: Bind issue preparation to preview generation, not issue creation

accepted_payload.body: Bind github.pr.comment.prepare to an adapter operation that gathers bounded pull request context and prepares a comment preview. The binding should require owner, repo, pull_number, comment_purpose, and context, with optional visibility and bounded backend_options. It must stop at preview and approval request output and must not post a comment on the preparation path.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source and runtime governance both define this capability as preview-only, approval-gated, and not a direct posting operation.
accepted_payload.title: Bind pull request comment preparation to preview-only comment drafting

accepted_payload.body: Bind github.workflow.dispatch.request to an adapter operation that validates repository, workflow_id, ref, and optional inputs, then produces a workflow dispatch preview or request record for approval. The binding should include restricted-workflow evaluation before any downstream dispatch path is eligible. It must deny workflow bypass attempts and must not dispatch on the request-stage binding itself.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline explicitly requires approval gating, explicit grant handling for restricted workflows, and denial of workflow bypass through backend options or direct backend access.
accepted_payload.title: Bind workflow dispatch to request-stage preparation with restricted-workflow checks

accepted_payload.body: Bind github.release_notes.prepare to adapter operations that gather bounded issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data for an explicit repository and range. The binding should support optional audience, include_prs, and bounded backend_options, and return a draft only. It must not create or publish a GitHub release.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source explicitly defines release-note generation as draft-only and backed by bounded repository context rather than release publication.
accepted_payload.title: Bind release-note preparation to bounded repository context aggregation

accepted_payload.body: Add a shared backend binding rule that required context validation happens in the GitHub Governance Service before adapter invocation. Missing owner, repo, query, title, body, pull_number, workflow_id, ref, or range should return clarification_required with the minimum missing fields, and no downstream GitHub call should occur until the request is complete.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and scenarios consistently require clarification_required behavior across capabilities when repository-specific context is incomplete.
accepted_payload.title: Enforce pre-execution clarification gates in the service binding layer

accepted_payload.body: Define a shared binding guardrail block that rejects secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumps, arbitrary mutation payload passthrough, and direct workflow bypass before any GitHub adapter call is made. backend_options should be constrained to bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, or expand-like metadata only.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked requirements make these denials and backend_options constraints part of the governed contract, not optional adapter behavior.
accepted_payload.title: Apply contract-level denial and backend_options guardrails before adapter calls

accepted_payload.body: Require every governed backend interaction and governed stop condition to emit durable, searchable audit metadata. The binding should capture capability requested, repository scope, preview versus denial versus clarification versus approval versus execution outcome, backend operation used, and lineage fields including task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id.
accepted_payload.rationale: Audit durability, searchability, and lineage fields are explicit locked requirements, and the baseline requires audit evidence for previews, approvals, denials, and backend execution.
accepted_payload.title: Bind all GitHub adapter calls to durable audit and lineage capture

data.approval_posture_summary: Read-oriented repository search and inspection can proceed within bounded scope, while issue creation, pull request comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication must stop at explicit approval boundaries. Drafting and preview are allowed where the brief marks them as preview-only or draft-only, but execution is not automatic.

artifact_lock.reason: The brief provides sufficient product-level intent and governance posture to establish a stable PM baseline, pending explicit acceptance and later integration evidence for contract generation.

business_goals: Expose business-safe repository and issue capabilities instead of raw GitHub API operations.
Make allowed organization and repository scope explicit and enforceable.

Support repository inspection, issue and pull request context search, issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, workflow dispatch requests, and release-note drafting within bounded controls.
Require explicit approval before higher-impact mutations or execution steps.
Deny unsafe behaviors such as secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and workflow bypass.
Preserve approval records and audit metadata for governed actions.
data.business_problem: Raw GitHub tools are powerful but too broad for safe agent use. Teams need a governed fronting layer that limits repository scope, makes approval requirements explicit, blocks unsafe operations, and preserves auditability while still enabling common repository and delivery tasks.
data.governed_behavior_summary: The product should answer only through bounded repository capabilities within explicit allowlisted scope. It should return summaries or drafts rather than raw repository export, ask for clarification when repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, stop before executing issue creation, PR comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without approval, and deny unsafe requests such as secret access, unbounded export, raw file exfiltration, or workflow bypass. Governed actions should remain auditable.
data.product_purpose: Provide business-safe, governed access to GitHub repository and delivery operations through bounded capabilities that let users inspect repository context, search issues and pull requests, prepare changes for review, request approved actions, and draft release notes without exposing raw code-hosting tools directly to agents.
supported_question_families: Issue and pull request search summary

data.policy_summary: GitHub access should be governed through bounded ANIP capabilities rather than raw GitHub APIs. Repository scope must be explicit and allowlisted. Repository context search and release-note drafting return bounded results only. Issue preparation and pull-request comment preparation are preview-only and require approval before any downstream mutation. Workflow dispatch requests are approval-gated, and restricted workflows require explicit grant. Missing repository, pull request, workflow, or range context returns clarification_required. Secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, and direct workflow bypass are denied. Audit records and approval records must be explicit.

accepted_payload.body: Require explicit repository-specific inputs before execution or preview generation. For repository search require owner, repo, and query. For issue preparation require owner, repo, title, and body. For pull request comment preparation require owner, repo, pull_number, comment_purpose, and context. For workflow dispatch requests require owner, repo, workflow_id, and ref. For release-note drafting require owner, repo, and range. If required repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, ambiguous, or unresolved, return clarification_required and do not execute backend operations. Do not infer missing scope or widen repository context implicitly. Enforce repository allowlists before any backend call or preview generation.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline repeatedly requires explicit repository scope, consistent clarification_required handling, and allowlist enforcement across the governed GitHub surface.
accepted_payload.title: Bind explicit repository scope and clarification_required behavior across all capabilities

accepted_payload.body: Treat issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, and workflow dispatch request as governed prepare-only flows. Runtime policy should allow generation of a reviewable preview or request artifact, produce approval.request and preview-oriented outputs, and explicitly block approval.execute and downstream mutation until approval is granted. Issue preparation must stop before issue creation. Pull request comment preparation must stop before posting. Workflow dispatch requests must stop before dispatch. Approval state and repository scope must remain attached to the runtime outcome for later execution and audit.
accepted_payload.rationale: The runtime governance evidence and source specification define these capabilities as preview-only or approval-gated and explicitly prohibit direct mutation without approval.
accepted_payload.title: Preserve preview-first and approval-gated mutation boundaries

accepted_payload.body: For workflow dispatch requests, apply a second policy check after repository allowlist validation to determine whether the requested workflow is restricted. Restricted workflows must require explicit grant handling beyond the base approval flow. If the request attempts to bypass workflow restrictions through backend_options, alternate workflow identifiers, or direct backend semantics, deny the request at the capability layer. Runtime outcomes should distinguish normal approval-gated workflow requests from restricted-workflow requests that need additional grant validation.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline explicitly calls out restricted workflows requiring explicit grant handling and denies direct workflow bypass, but does not fully specify the grant authority.
accepted_payload.title: Add stricter runtime handling for restricted workflows and workflow bypass attempts

accepted_payload.body: Define explicit deny outcomes for secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumps, arbitrary mutation payload passthrough, and direct workflow bypass. Denied requests must not be translated into raw GitHub REST or GraphQL calls. The runtime policy should preserve the bounded capability surface even when downstream GitHub APIs could technically perform broader actions. Return a governed stop with denial metadata rather than partial unsafe output.
accepted_payload.rationale: Unsafe and out-of-bounds operations are explicitly denied in the locked baseline and denial is required to happen at the ANIP contract layer, not only in backend behavior.
accepted_payload.title: Deny unsafe GitHub operations at the contract layer

accepted_payload.body: Allow backend_options only for bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, GraphQL pagination, or expand-like metadata. Runtime policy must reject backend_options that attempt arbitrary mutation payloads, secret access, workflow bypass, unbounded extraction, or other raw backend control. Apply the same guardrail consistently across repository search, issue preparation, pull request comment preparation, workflow dispatch requests, and release-note drafting.
accepted_payload.rationale: The source specification and requirements explicitly allow backend_options only as bounded provider controls and identify them as a potential escape hatch that must be closed.
accepted_payload.title: Constrain backend_options to bounded provider features only

accepted_payload.body: For repository search, runtime policy should permit bounded issue and pull request summaries only and prohibit raw repository export or unbounded data return. For release-note drafting, runtime policy should permit bounded aggregation from issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data and return a draft only. Release-note preparation must not create or publish a release. Both capabilities should remain read-oriented even when backed by multiple GitHub data sources.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline defines repository search as bounded summary access and release-note generation as draft-only without release publication.
accepted_payload.title: Bind read-only and draft-only output posture for search and release-note capabilities

accepted_payload.body: Attach durable audit metadata to every governed runtime outcome, including requested capability, repository scope, whether the request was previewed, clarified, denied, approved, or executed, and which backend GitHub operation was used. Preserve lineage fields including task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id. Audit records should make approval boundaries and denied or clarified stops reviewable after the fact.
accepted_payload.rationale: The locked baseline requires durable searchable audit, explicit outcome recording, backend operation traceability, and lineage preservation.
accepted_payload.title: Record audit and lineage metadata for previews, approvals, denials, clarifications, and execution

accepted_payload.body: Runtime policy should declare native GitHub REST and GraphQL operations as the only execution binding for this package. MCP may be referenced for comparison but must not be treated as the runtime execution interface or as an alternate path around governed capability semantics. All governed outcomes should resolve through the centralized GitHub fronting service and its native GitHub adapter path.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline explicitly states that native GitHub REST/GraphQL is the execution binding and that MCP is not the required ANIP interface.
accepted_payload.title: Bind runtime execution to native GitHub REST and GraphQL integrations only

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: repository_operator bounded access to Repository Context Access should return bounded_result. Allow bounded issue and pull-request context search within an explicit allowlisted repository. Return summaries only, not raw repository export. Supported by github.repo.search_context and the read-only bounded search posture.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Allow bounded issue and pull-request context search within an explicit allowlisted repository. Return summaries only, not raw repository export.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: issue_requester approval_required access to Issue Preparation and Creation Governance should return approval_stop. Allow issue preparation preview with labels and assignees, but stop for approval before any issue is created downstream. Preview-only posture from github.issue.prepare; creation is not executed without approval.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Allow issue preparation preview with labels and assignees, but stop for approval before any issue is created downstream.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: pr_comment_requester approval_required access to Pull Request Comment Governance should return approval_stop. Allow pull-request comment preparation preview from review or release context, but stop for approval before posting any comment. Preview-only posture from github.pr.comment.prepare; posting is not executed without approval.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Allow pull-request comment preparation preview from review or release context, but stop for approval before posting any comment.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: workflow_requester approval_required access to Workflow Dispatch Governance should return approval_stop. Allow a workflow dispatch request to be prepared for an allowlisted repository and ref, but require approval before dispatch. Restricted workflows require explicit grant. Supported by github.workflow.dispatch.request and policy semantics for restricted workflows.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Allow a workflow dispatch request to be prepared for an allowlisted repository and ref, but require approval before dispatch. Restricted workflows require explicit grant.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: release_notes_drafter bounded access to Release Notes Drafting should return bounded_result. Allow draft release notes from bounded repository context only. Do not create or publish a release. Supported by github.release_notes.prepare and the draft-only posture.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Allow draft release notes from bounded repository context only. Do not create or publish a release.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: repository_operator denied access to Governed Repository Policy and Audit should return deny_request. Deny requests for secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, or direct workflow bypass. Cross-cutting denial posture stated in policy semantics.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Deny requests for secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded codebase dumps, or direct workflow bypass.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: repository_operator restricted access to Repository Context Access should return clarification_required. Stop and request clarification when repository context is missing or repository scope is not explicit. The source requires explicit repository scope and clarification on missing context.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Stop and request clarification when repository context is missing or repository scope is not explicit.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: pr_comment_requester restricted access to Pull Request Comment Governance should return clarification_required. Stop and request clarification when pull request context is missing. The source explicitly calls out missing pull request context as clarification_required.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Stop and request clarification when pull request context is missing.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: workflow_requester restricted access to Workflow Dispatch Governance should return clarification_required. Stop and request clarification when workflow context is missing. The source explicitly calls out missing workflow context as clarification_required.
permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Stop and request clarification when workflow context is missing.

permission_intent_bindings.formalization_strategy: release_notes_drafter restricted access to Release Notes Drafting should return clarification_required. permission_intent_bindings.governed_outcome: Stop and request clarification when release-note range context is missing.

product_alignment.approval_posture_formalization: Read-oriented repository search and inspection can proceed within bounded scope, while issue creation, pull request comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication must stop at explicit approval boundaries. Drafting and preview are allowed where the brief marks them as preview-only or draft-only, but execution is not automatic.
product_alignment.governed_behavior_formalization: The product should answer only through bounded repository capabilities within explicit allowlisted scope. It should return summaries or drafts rather than raw repository export, ask for clarification when repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, stop before executing issue creation, PR comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without approval, and deny unsafe requests such as secret access, unbounded export, raw file exfiltration, or workflow bypass. Governed actions should remain auditable.

service_topology_bindings.implementation_notes:
Accepted assistant guidance:
- Formalize the capability surface each service must expose: Define the stable capability contracts each service is expected to own, including which actions are read-only, which require approval, and which must stop for clarification.
  Why: The generator and verifier need stable capability ownership, not only narrative service descriptions.
- Describe the required coordination edges between services: Capture where one service depends on another for bounded context, approvals, or downstream execution so coordination can be represented explicitly instead of as hidden glue.
  Why: Coordination edges are easier to validate when they are declared before implementation.
- Keep approval boundaries outside the main happy path: Identify which service should prepare a proposed action and which surface or role must approve it before the mutation boundary is crossed.
  Why: Approval semantics should be explicit in service design rather than buried in one implementation path.
- Preserve explanation and traceability across service boundaries: Define which service boundaries must preserve explanation, lineage, or durable evidence so later verification can reconstruct why a result or stop condition occurred.
  Why: Cross-service traceability should be deliberate, not inferred after implementation exists.

product_alignment.approval_posture_formalization: Read-oriented repository search and inspection can proceed within bounded scope, while issue creation, pull request comments, workflow dispatches, and release publication must stop at explicit approval boundaries. Drafting and preview are allowed where the brief marks them as preview-only or draft-only, but execution is not automatic.
product_alignment.governed_behavior_formalization: The product should answer only through bounded repository capabilities within explicit allowlisted scope. It should return summaries or drafts rather than raw repository export, ask for clarification when repository, pull request, workflow, or range context is missing, stop before executing issue creation, PR comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without approval, and deny unsafe requests such as secret access, unbounded export, raw file exfiltration, or workflow bypass. Governed actions should remain auditable.

service_topology_bindings.implementation_notes:
Accepted assistant guidance:
- Formalize the capability surface each service must expose: Define the stable capability contracts each service is expected to own, including which actions are read-only, which require approval, and which must stop for clarification.
  Why: The generator and verifier need stable capability ownership, not only narrative service descriptions.
- Describe the required coordination edges between services: Capture where one service depends on another for bounded context, approvals, or downstream execution so coordination can be represented explicitly instead of as hidden glue.
  Why: Coordination edges are easier to validate when they are declared before implementation.
- Keep approval boundaries outside the main happy path: Identify which service should prepare a proposed action and which surface or role must approve it before the mutation boundary is crossed.
  Why: Approval semantics should be explicit in service design rather than buried in one implementation path.
- Preserve explanation and traceability across service boundaries: Define which service boundaries must preserve explanation, lineage, or durable evidence so later verification can reconstruct why a result or stop condition occurred.
  Why: Cross-service traceability should be deliberate, not inferred after implementation exists.

business_constraints.assistant_drafted_requirements_summary:
Expose a governed GitHub capability surface instead of raw GitHub operations: The product shall provide an ANIP fronting service for GitHub that exposes business-safe repository capabilities rather than raw GitHub REST, GraphQL, or tool-level operations. The contract shall own the capability semantics presented to agents and callers, while native GitHub APIs remain the backend integration surface.
Support bounded repository context search and inspection within explicit repository scope: The product shall support read-only repository context access for allowed repositories, including bounded issue and pull request search using explicit repository inputs such as owner, repo, and query. Results shall return bounded summaries rather than raw repository export or unbounded codebase data.
Provide issue preparation as a preview-first flow with governed inputs: The product shall allow a caller to prepare a GitHub issue using explicit repository scope and issue fields, including title and body, with governed optional fields such as labels, assignees, and milestone. The preparation step shall return a preview suitable for review and approval rather than directly creating the issue.
Require explicit approval before any governed GitHub mutation is executed: The product shall not execute issue creation, pull request comment posting, workflow dispatch, or release publication without explicit approval. For mutation requests, the system shall stop at a reviewable preview or request state until approval is granted, and shall preserve the approval boundary in the contract behavior.
Support governed pull request comment preparation without direct posting: The product shall support preparation of pull request comments from review or release context using explicit repository and pull request identifiers. The output shall be a preview-only comment draft, with posting blocked until approval is granted.
Gate workflow dispatch requests and restrict sensitive workflows: The product shall support requesting a GitHub Actions workflow run using explicit repository, workflow, and ref inputs, but shall treat dispatch as approval-gated. Workflows designated as restricted shall require explicit grant handling beyond the base request flow, and the system shall not allow workflow bypass through backend options or direct backend access.
Generate release notes as bounded drafts without publishing releases: The product shall support drafting release notes from bounded repository context such as issue, pull request, milestone, or comparison data using an explicit release range. The capability shall produce draft output only and shall not create or publish a GitHub release.
Return clarification_required when required repository or operation context is missing: The product shall return clarification_required when a request lacks the repository, pull request, workflow, or release range context needed to safely execute the governed capability. The system shall not infer missing scope or proceed with partial context for governed operations.
Deny unsafe or out-of-bounds GitHub operations at the contract level: The product shall explicitly deny secret exfiltration, raw file export, unbounded repository export, unbounded codebase dumps, arbitrary mutation payload passthrough, and direct workflow bypass. These denials shall be part of the governed contract rather than left to backend behavior alone.
Constrain backend options to bounded provider features only: If backend_options are exposed, they shall be limited to bounded provider features such as selected fields, pagination, or expand-like metadata. backend_options shall not permit arbitrary mutation payloads, secret access, workflow bypass, or unbounded data extraction.
Make repository allowlists and enterprise control posture explicit: The product shall make allowed organization and repository scope explicit and enforce it consistently across all governed capabilities. The service design shall reflect centralized enterprise identity, repository allowlists, approval records, and audit expectations as first-class control mechanisms.
Record audit metadata for previews, approvals, denials, and backend execution: The product shall preserve audit metadata that allows reviewers to understand what capability was requested, what repository scope applied, whether the request was previewed, approved, denied, clarified, or executed, and which backend GitHub operation was used. Audit behavior shall be explicit in the contract and service expectations.
Bind execution to native GitHub REST and GraphQL integrations, not MCP: The product shall use native GitHub REST and GraphQL APIs as the execution binding for this package. MCP may be referenced only as a comparison point for raw tool exposure and shall not be treated as the required ANIP execution interface.
